summary.brief: Burks v. Allen. Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: Decedent was admitted to University of Maryland Medical Center (UMMC) with multi-system diagnoses, including renal and liver failure.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Burks treated him for presumed hyperkalemia, which was later confirmed.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The treatment included Kayexalate given in a suspension with sorbitol and hemodialysis.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Shortly thereafter decedent developed ischemic colitis which quickly progressed to necrosis of the colon.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The plaintiffs’ expert witnesses theorized that the decedent’s ischemic colitis was caused by the Kayexalate with sorbitol and opined that the standard of care required treatment with dialysis alone.
evidence_index.speaker: Plaintiffs' expert witnesses

evidence_index.text: Held: Judgment affirmed.
evidence_index.speaker: Court of Special Appeals

evidence_index.text: The Court of Special Appeals assumed without deciding that Frye-Reed applied to the proposed expert testimony and affirmed the trial court’s alternative ruling, made without holding an evidentiary hearing, that that testimony satisfied the Frye-Reed test.
evidence_index.speaker: Court of Special Appeals

evidence_index.text: On March 10, 2013, Dennis Allen, age 63, was transported by ambulance to Northwest Hospital Center in Randallstown for complaints of increasing '[w]eakness of the arms and legs.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: He was suffering from hepatitis C, cirrhosis of the liver, end stage liver disease, renal failure, and congestive heart failure.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Blood tests performed at Northwest Hospital Center revealed that Mr. Allen also was suffering from acute rhabdomyolysis.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Mr. Allen was transferred from Northwest Hospital Center to UMMC the next day and was admitted to the intermediate care unit.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Burks was the attending physician assigned to him.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On March 18, 2013, Dr. Burks arrived at UMMC sometime between 7 a.m. and 8 a.m.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Shortly after noon, Mr. Allen experienced a precipitous drop in heart rate, setting off the heart monitor alarms.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Burks ordered an immediate EKG, which was performed at 12:18 p.m. It showed bradycardia (an abnormally slow heart rhythm) and life-threatening heart rhythms.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Burks made a preliminary diagnosis of hyperkalemia, i.e., an elevated level of potassium in the blood.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Goldstein opined that Mr. Allen died from intestinal necrosis caused by the lining of the colon [to] stop working.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Goldstein

evidence_index.text: Dr. Leo also testified that Dr. Burks breached the standard of care by not obtaining informed consent.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Leo

evidence_index.text: The jury returned a verdict in favor of the Allens, awarding $2,000,000 in non-economic damages to the Estate, and $1,000,000 in non-economic damages to Mr. Allen’s wife and each of his seven children, for a total of $10,000,000.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: JUDGMENT AFFIRMED. COSTS TO BE PAID BY THE APPELLANTS.
evidence_index.speaker: Court of Special Appeals

initial_state.chief_complaint: Weakness of the arms and legs
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient with history of hepatitis C, cirrhosis of the liver, end stage liver disease, renal failure, and congestive heart failure presented with increasing weakness. Blood tests revealed acute rhabdomyolysis.

requestables.name: Blood Potassium Level
requestables.description: Measurement of potassium level in blood

reveal.decision_impact: Requires immediate treatment to prevent cardiac arrhythmia

reveal.clinical_significance: Dialysis is effective in removing potassium
reveal.decision_impact: Dialysis should be prioritized over Kayexalate

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with weakness and is transferred to UMMC

timeline_phases.name: Workup and Initial Treatment
timeline_phases.description: Patient experiences bradycardia and is treated for hyperkalemia

timeline_phases.name: Postoperative and Complications
timeline_phases.description: Patient develops ischemic colitis and undergoes surgery

key_events.event: Patient experiences bloody stools and abdominal pain

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient with confirmed hyperkalemia and bradycardia
decision_points.prompt: What treatment should be initiated for hyperkalemia?

options.clinical_reasoning: Dialysis is effective and avoids risk of ischemic colitis from Kayexalate

options.description: Administer Kayexalate with sorbitol and hemodialysis
options.clinical_reasoning: Kayexalate poses risk of ischemic colitis, especially with sorbitol

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant administered Kayexalate with sorbitol and hemodialysis
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Believed Kayexalate would provide prolonged potassium removal

expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated dialysis alone was sufficient and safer

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Kayexalate with sorbitol is associated with risk of ischemic colitis
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Dialysis should have been used exclusively

decision_points.prompt: Should informed consent be obtained before administering Kayexalate?

options.description: Obtain informed consent before administering Kayexalate
options.clinical_reasoning: Patient should be informed of risks, especially potential for ischemic colitis

options.description: Do not obtain informed consent due to emergency
options.clinical_reasoning: Believed emergency nature negated need for consent

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant did not obtain informed consent

expected_action_court.description: Informed consent should have been obtained
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Patient should be informed of significant risks

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to inform patient of risks associated with Kayexalate
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Patient should have been informed and consent obtained

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed ischemic colitis leading to necrosis and death

key_findings: Breach of duty by administering Kayexalate with sorbitol
Causation established - treatment caused ischemic colitis and death

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Administration of Kayexalate with sorbitol without informed consent
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Without sorbitol, risk of ischemic colitis would have been reduced

factual_timeline.event: Patient transported to Northwest Hospital Center
Patient develops bloody stools and abdominal pain
Patient arrives at Northwest Hospital Center

court_findings.finding: Defendant breached duty of care by administering Kayexalate with sorbitol
court_findings.legal_significance: Breach of standard of care and causation established
